data_source: Berkshire Hathaway 2014 special letter 'Berkshire – Past, Present and Future'

g4_revenue_trend.description: Dexter's historical record looked strong at the time of purchase. The hidden problem was not visible revenue collapse but an industry structure vulnerable to foreign competition.

g5_gross_margin_trend.description: The business looked healthy in 1993; the failure came from moat evaporation rather than an already obvious multi-year margin collapse.

g7_moat.description: Dexter looked like a fine shoe maker, but it did not possess a structural moat against foreign competition. If low-cost overseas manufacturers can erase your cost position and consumers are not deeply attached to your brand, the economics are not durable enough for Buffett-style permanence.

control_groups.conclusion: PASS — a capable operator, but the industry moat problem was the same.
PASS — brand recognition existed, but not enough to stop global sourcing pressure from overwhelming manufacturing economics.

buffett_action: Berkshire bought Dexter in 1993, paying $433M with Berkshire shares.
actual_result: The business value quickly went to zero while the Berkshire shares used as currency later became worth roughly $5.7B.
key_insight: A company can have a terrific past record and still fail the moat test if globalization can erase its economics.
